messages.content: You are an expert EU proposal writer for IRIS Technology Solutions, a photonics and NIR spectroscopy company. Write in first-person plural (we/our). Use precise technical language. Follow EU Horizon proposal conventions: numbered tasks (**Task X.Y**), clear objectives, evidence-based claims. Present the business case, market analysis, commercialisation pathway, and investment needs.
Write the Business case and exploitation strategy section for a Horizon Europe proposal.

4. We will recognize the orientation of the caps with our vision system, which allows us to pick them up from the feeder. With our vacuum end effector, we populate the panel. 5. We will flip the panel upside-down, face down. 6. Our assembly process continues with us taking components from the automatic kitting warehouse and the operator taking components from the manual kitting warehouse (i.e. cables). 7. We will switch the end effector to the screwing head, allowing us to fix components like PCB using screws. 8. Once assembly is done, we will move the panel to the side where the test area is. The operator connects the panel, and we perform the test by pushing buttons while our vision system monitors the panel’s display to confirm proper functioning. 9. While the test is running, the operator can proceed with the assembly of the next panel. 10. When the test is finished, the operator removes the panel and places it on a cart, ready for packaging. In the SCHINDLER use case, we will demonstrate the features of the Collaborative by Birth Robotic Manipulator, delivering a low payload robotic arm for electronic components production, integrated with the other platform components (sensing system, controller, etc.). Given our user’s high production variability, the promised ROSSINI performance in terms of production reconfiguration cost savings will be tested. Use Case #3 – Food Products Packaging (IMA) The scenario we propose is a complex system for flexible, collaborative packaging material loading and handling on IMA tea machines in industrial shop floors. Our operational area is around 160 square meters. This scenario is basically composed of five components that are interacting within the environment by means of several kinds of command and control subsystems: Packaging machines; Processing workers; Raw materials; Finished products; Mobile cooperative robotic platforms. Our work flow is centered around the machine, able to build and package the finished products when properly fed and unloaded. In a conventional shop floor, we, the processing workers, are the people that have in charge the tasks to: 1. Choose the raw materials from the material warehouse. 2. Bring the chosen materials from the material warehouse to the feeding point on the machine. 3. Load in the right manner the raw materials on the machine. 4. Operate the process requests for loaded materials (manual tape joining, for example). 5. Verify the right parameter and setting of the machine. 6. Verify the unload from the machine and the product delivering. 7. Check the machine status to anticipate the need to load new raw material. Figure 13 - Use Case #2 TO BE Situation Figure 14 - Demo Case #3: AS-IS Machine load 25 [818087][ROSSINI] – Part B 8. Assure the work cycle efficacy by providing quick maintenance or rapid solution in case of small failure of the process (as an example: optical cleaning of photocells by blowing, clogged up bags removal, removal of the blocked external envelopes, blocked stacking removal). 9. Format change by material removal and replacement of mechanical parts. In the prospected solution, we will cooperatively share the previous list of tasks between human workers and robot platforms and related technologies. The solution will enable cooperative operation of the material feeding process on two or more machines for tea bags packaging operations. Our system will enable us to robustly identify distinct types of packaging material, as well as analyze and predict the need for several types of loading operations on distinct machines at the shop floor. Besides being able to conduct pick and place operations, we will also safely navigate on the shop floor environment together with humans. Our interaction with humans will be considered both for safety because the environment where the transportation of the material is performed is in a space shared with human operators, and also to enable direct interaction between us and the robotized platform to make some tasks (and their customization) more efficient. The sharing of tasks could be devised as follows: 1. Robot: Choose the raw materials from the material warehouse. 2. Robot: Bring the chosen materials from the material warehouse to the feeding point on the machine. 3. Robot: Load in the right manner the raw materials on the machine. 4. Robots: Operate the process requests for loaded materials (manual tape joining, for example). 5. (Worker and Robot): Verify the right parameter and setting of the machine. 6. (Worker and Robot): Verify the unload from the machine and the product delivering. 7. (Worker and Robot): Check the machine status to anticipate the need to load new raw material. 8. (Worker and Robot): Assure the work cycle efficacy by providing quick maintenance or rapid solution in case of small failure of the process (as an example: optical cleaning of photocells by blowing, clogged up bags removal, removal of the blocked external envelopes, blocked stacking removal). 9. (Worker and Robot): Format change by material removal and replacement of mechanical parts. In the IMA use case, we will deploy a medium payload manipulator mounted on an AGV (mobile robotic platform). We will concentrate on Safety Laser Scanners, whereas the Human Layer and the Control Architecture will be confronted with the challenge to manage navigation operations as well as other operations (screwing, etc.). Being IMA also a robot integrator, our demonstrator will be easily replicated. ROSSINI Use Cases Synoptic Table Features Domestic Appliances Electronic Components Food Products HRC Operations Pick & Place, Mounting Pick & Place, Screwing Navigation, Reel Feeding, Screwing Safeguarding Modes Implemented Power & Force Limiting, Hand Guiding Safety Rated Monitored Stop, Power & Force Limiting Safety Rated Monitored Stop, Power & Force Limiting, Hand Guiding Manipulator Third party medium payload cobot (30kg) Low Payload ROSSINI Cobot (>5kg) Mobile ROSSINI Cobot – Medium Payload (20Kg) Achievable collaboration Collaboration Parallel Cooperation Collaboration (Feeding), Parallel Cooperation (Navigation) Figure 15 - Use Case #3 TO BE Configuration 26 [818087][ROSSINI] – Part B Gender dimension We will adopt practical methods for sex and gender analysis as a resource to develop novel HRC workstation design. Our four lines of research (control, sensing, actuation, Human-robot mutual understanding) will take into account the integration of sex/gender issues from the setting of research priorities through developing methodologies, gathering and analyzing data, to evaluating and reporting results and transferring them to markets. During the development of our project, we will incorporate knowledge about sex and gender into engineering innovation processes in order to design HRC workstations that promote human well-being, including gender equality, and develop technologies that meet the needs of a complex and diverse user group. 1.4 Ambition 1.4.1 Progress beyond the state of the art Smart and Safe Sensing System Today our robot work area is usually monitored only on its perimeter and mainly for safety reasons. In every instance of our use, safety sensors are intended to do two things: monitor specific regions for operator presence and signal the equipment to slow or stop when the operator's presence is detected. For industrial applications, sensitive protective equipment (SPE) for the detection of the presence of human beings may include both Electro-Sensitive Protective Equipment (ESPE) such as laser curtains, laser scanners, and vision-based safety systems e.g. SafetyEye, and Pressure-Sensitive Protective Equipment (PSPE) such as pressure-sensitive mats, etc. Typically, the selection of the equipment as part of the safety-related system depends on the application. SPE are designed to monitor a predefined space and need to be triggered to be shut down or stopped. Therefore, the positioning and installation dictate a minimum distance that needs to be maintained from the hazardous zone. When triggered, these sensors use electrical safety signals, and each single safety sensor decides if a safety reaction is needed. Therefore, only simple Boolean operations are done between the results of different sensors. Non-standardized sensors such as passive infrared thermal protective devices are permitted for use as part of a larger safety system, but without an associated performance standard, they are neither considered reliable enough nor suitable as the sole means of protection. Subsequently, such non-standardized sensors must be accompanied by primary, safety-rated sensing equipment (Marvel. J et al. 2014). In addition, in our robotic workcell, vision systems are often used for object localization and robot guidance, as they deliver high-precision measurement results and consequently play a key role in making part handling (e.g. SICK) and robot guiding, and tactile sensors are used on our robot’s end-effector to control the grasping force and allow a correct and stable grip of the objects. However, this kind of sensors are very different from safety sensors, which are designed to slow down the robot once the worker is in a certain area/space and to stop it once the worker is too close. Moreover, even in collaborative workcells, our robot movements are computed based on predefined and optimized trajectories, and some areas are forbidden for safety reasons, particularly to avoid collision (e.g. Safe Mode Robot Safety Option ABB). In industrial applications, safe areas around robots are defined at setup time using complex tools and procedures, thus limiting flexibility because even a complex sensor system can guarantee security but cannot provide a real-time univocal multidimensional semantic scene map. Moreover, the necessity of a stringent risk assessment is usually transformed into conservative speed limitations or bounds on driving torques of robots. Yet, even if our robot’s internal components and software have been certified, a risk assessment certification is required for the whole application, and a new certification is needed every time the system changes. ROSSINI innovation We aim to design a Smart and Safe Sensing System with improved detection and tracking capabilities for monitoring the entire

Follow EU Horizon proposal conventions: numbered tasks (**Task X.Y**), clear objectives, evidence-based claims. Write the Business case and exploitation strategy section for a Horizon Europe proposal.

4. VALUE PROPOSITION The innovation: We will industrially deploy a unique smart, dynamic, disruptive, and multi-product biorefinery that will transform the plant-based ingredients sector. This first of its kind model will enable us to maximize the obtained value out of processed feedstocks with an optimal cascading use of bio-based feedstock, aiming to achieve “zero waste” and “zero-pollution” operations. Moreover, our project will showcase a first-of its kind model easily replicable by the whole European bio-based industry. This new model is adaptable to each type and quality of feedstock and able to switch to renewable energy sources. All in all, resulting in an improved environmental performing biorefinery, with maximum resource and energy-efficiency. The model is based on the integration of the whole supply chain - from feedstock to end users - applying a disruptive circular model based on a smart dynamic analytical biorefinery (DYANA). This system is a smart dynamic biorefining process for the cascade valorisation (12 valorisation routes - VR) of feedstocks. Each production process is optimized batch-wise according to the initial composition of the feedstock (feedstock suitability index – FSI) to deliver maximum value with minimum environmental impact and ensuring resource efficiency. DYANA will allow us to achieve the complete valorisation of the processed feedstock with a zero-waste and zero-emissions ambition and deliver 46 plant-based healthy extracts and functional ingredients. DYANA has been developed with a novel concept based on several aspects: Figure 1.2 Main concepts of SUSTAINEXT The team: This challenge requires us to build up a prosperous team covering the whole value chain and with demonstrated technological development. We have put together a competitive highly specialised consortium covering the whole value chain. Agro-industrial biomass will be locally sourced by INNO (olive), AGROS (artichoke and pomegranate), DOP (cardoon); MAPs will be sustainably cultivated by TABEX, DOP, INP and CTAEX; MAPs cultivation in agrivoltaics will be developed by ENEL; ARRAM and NATAC will be in charge of the biorefinery design and construction; sensor technology development will be provided by IRIS; market uptake will be fostered by end users within our consortium NUT (nutraceuticals), STAR (food), ACEL (pets), MIAVIT (feed additives), MBL (cosmetics) and BIOM (fertilizers and biostimulants); sustainability assessment will be done by ECO; and D&C and multifactor approach will be performed by BIOPLAT and FUNCYT. The feedstocks: We will industrially deploy our project with six sustainably and locally sourced European feedstocks: three agro-industrial side-streams (olive, artichoke/cardoon, and pomegranate) and three medicinal and Aromatic Plants (MAPs) (camomile, rosemary, and lemon verbena). 10,000 tonnes are foreseen to be used in year 5-after project during exploitation phase. 10 Echemi, 2022. Overview of the Global Botanical Extracts Market Under Today Economic Model. Published 18-03-2022. Accessed 13-09-2022. - Page 5 of 70 - Olive biomass will be locally supplied by INNO, a joint venture between Natac and Olei cola El Tejar the largest olive biomass manager in the world (15 % of total world olive biomass). - Pomegranate and artichoke side-streams will be locally supplied by AGROS, a biomass operator closely linked with Spanish agroindustry. - Cardoon side-streams from cheese coagulant production will be locally supplied by DOP Torta del Casar. - MAPs plants will be grown in disused tobacco fields by TABEX and in agrivoltaics system by ENEL. The flagship: Such innovation does not start from scratch, but instead will be built upon our company’s already innovative manufacturing ISO 14001 certified plant in Hervás, Extremadura. The current factory has an extraction capacity of 3,000 tons/year and was built in 2020. It has a modern and efficient unit for polyphenol production equipped with extraction, concentration, aqueous phase purification, spray-dry drying and clean room. We aim to design and scale up new equipment to be integrated with the existing facility to deploy a disruptive dynamic biorefinery able to address the newly defined processes and integrate the plant as a whole to scale up the DYANA concept. Moreover, a circular by design will be implemented to achieve a NetZero status and a zero pollution and zero waste ambition. The main aim is to convert a modern state of the art polyphenols production plant into a first-of-its kind dynamic multiproduct plant unique in the plant-based ingredients sector worldwide. Our project will not only enable us to be more efficient, but also to manufacture new products and extracts in a multiproduct side-stream methodology, and to become a zero-waste and zero-pollution company, getting ahead of the future of the industry and lining up with consumer demands. The products: We will use 6 feedstocks to develop 12 valorisation routes (fig 1.8) to yield 46 plant-based healthy extracts and functional ingredients out of which 13 are totally new in the world and 12 are newly produced in Europe (currently imported from APAC, mainly China). 24 of these ingredients belong to the extensive polyphenol family, 14 are terpenoids and the rest to other chemical families. These products will be used in target high prized markets: food supplements (NUT), feed additives (MIAVIT), food ingredients (STAR), pet additives (ACEL), cosmetics (MBL) and performance chemicals (NATAC). As mentioned, we combine state of the art commodities with a solid presence in the market and most innovative ingredients. This will warranty short term sales right after the flagship launch while entering the market with more innovative products. Brand owners within our consortium: As mentioned, we are a solid company with a consolidated B2B position in the market. However, in order to maximise the market entrance of newly developed products, we include recognized brand owners from target sectors: food supplements (Nutraresearch), food industry (GB Foods), feed additive industry (MIAVIT), pet additive market (ACEL) and cosmetic (MIBELLE). 5. IMPACTS - We will position ourselves at the forefront of the plant-based ingredients industry breaking the most important market barriers, while achieving a NetZero status. - A revenue of 194.71 M€ in year 5 is forecasted with a net profit of 94.11 M€. - 46 plant-based healthy extracts and functional ingredients with market prize between 20 - 300 €/kg. - A new technology (DYANA algorithm) will dramatically change the way cascade biorefining is approached. - Our project will be highly replicable in biobased Europe. - Feedstocks providers within our consortium will benefit from new business opportunities. - End users will deliver cost-effective, sustainable, and high-quality new formulations to the market. - The regional impact in Extremadura will be decisive in terms of socioeconomic development (job creation, investment attraction, new value chains unlocking). - It will create more than 6,300 new green jobs (1,500 direct and 4,800 indirect and induced). We have received institutional support at all levels. Mr. Guillermo Fernández Vara (President of Extremadura), Ms. Begoña García (Regional Minister for Agriculture, Rural Development, Population and Territory Councillor of Agriculture) and Ms. Patricia Valle (Mayor of Hervás) have signed letters where they show the strong commitment of the region towards this challenging project. Moreover, we have received additional support from investors, local community, end users, civil associations, among others. A final summary lists the supporting letters received. - Page 6 of 70 1.1.1. Objective Overall objective Our main general objective is to scale up and validate a first-of-its-kind industrial facility based on the integration of the whole supply chain - from feedstock providers to product’s end-users - applying a disruptive circular model integrated in a dynamic biorefinery, to achieve the complete valorisation of sustainably sourced feedstocks (industrial by-products olive, artichoke and pomegranate and medicinal and aromatic plants camomile, rosemary, and lemon verbena), with a zero-waste and zero-emissions ambition to deliver 46 plant-based healthy extracts and functional ingredients, while transforming a region with an outdated cropping system into a cutting edge bio-based circular referent. Specific objectives - SO1: To consolidate a close collaboration link with the agri-food industry to establish a solid supply chain for the upcycling of agro-industrial side streams. Means of verification WP1 Partial objectives - To scale, optimize and validate at TRL 8 rosemary, lemon verbena and camomile crops cultivation at a hectare scale in disused tobacco fields. KPI: Each plant: Production of 6 batches/2MT each - To scale, optimize and validate at TRL 8 rosemary crops in agrivoltaics. KPI: Production of 6 batches/2MT each - To establish a stable and sustainable supply of aerial part of cardoon from DOP ‘Torta del Casar’. KPI: Production of 6 batches/1MT each - To demonstrate at TRL -8 the relevance of applying pre-treatments on the feedstocks to modulate the FSI and their impact in the value chain. KPI, improvement of 50% FSI D1.1 to D1.8 Milestone M6 and M10 SO2: To deploy a large scale (TRL 8) unique smart dynamic disruptive and multi-product digital biore

You are an expert EU proposal writer for IRIS Technology Solutions, a photonics and NIR spectroscopy company. Write in first-person plural (we/our). Use precise technical language. Follow EU Horizon proposal conventions: numbered tasks (**Task X.Y**), clear objectives, evidence-based claims. Present the business case, market analysis, commercialisation pathway, and investment needs.
Write the Business case and exploitation strategy section for a Horizon Europe proposal.

1 Excellence #@REL-EV A-RE@# 1.1 Objectives and Ambition W2W Motivation and Strategic Vision: We recognize that wood is a natural, renewable, and biodegradable material with impressive mechanical and thermal properties. According to future projections, due to its increasing use for energy production, and in products such as building materials and chemicals, our wood production may be insufficient to meet European demand by 20301. At the same time, we see an increasing volume of wood contained in Construction & Demolition (C&D) waste (20-30% of the total building-related C&D) and furniture waste (10 million tonnes of furniture are discarded by businesses and consumers in EU Member States each year Associated with document Ref. Ares(2023)8305270 - 05/12/20232). Recycling these streams can provide us with an abundant and cost-effective raw material source. In addition, our waste wood streams (from industrial and urban waste) contain significant amounts of fossil-based carbon from coatings, paints, plastics, and preservatives, which we could recycle using innovative processes (among others, chemical recycling), making their valorisation one of our priorities in the Processes4Planet (P4P) partnership for wood waste processing. However, we acknowledge that recycling processes are greatly complicated by the presence of additives (glues, varnishes, and paints), pollutants (wood treatment products 1 Alex Forrest, Mark Hilton, Ann Ballinger & Daniel Whittaker, Circular Economy Opportunities in the Furniture Sector, European Environment Bureau, (2017). [W2W][101138789][ HORIZON-CL4-2023-TWIN-TRANSITION-01-42] 3 and heavy metals), and contaminating materials (glass, plastics, metals, etc.), and further research and development are needed to improve them. Other challenges we face include the irregular and unpredictable availability of such waste streams, the complexity of sorting and cleaning processes, and the gaps in existing regulations which may not fully support the use of upcycled wood materials. As a result, our current wood waste management strategies still prioritise landfill and energy recovery over material recovery2. To tackle these challenges, we propose a comprehensive framework for multi-dimensional cascade4 valorisation of wood from C&D and furniture waste, consisting of four core components/pillars: 1) Advanced Separation and Sorting Technologies; 2) Upcycling Processes and Technologies; 3) Digital Tools for Improving Circular Flows of Secondary Materials; and 4) Supportive Framework in Policy, Market, and Skills. We will demonstrate efficient and sustainable value chains for the production of: wood without pollutants; biocomposite building materials; biopolymers; polyols; chemical detergents; and the recovery of nutrients, through the use of technologies and tools that allow us to select optimal cascade paths for further uses of wood products and their materials. Our holistic approach will reduce the demand for virgin materials, reduce the amount of waste that ends up in landfills or incineration facilities, allow for the creation of new value-added products from waste materials, and support the transition towards a circular economy by promoting closed-loop systems where materials are continually repurposed and reused, extending their useful life. 1.1.1 W2W Objectives and Success Criteria O1: To produce and validate our comprehensive framework in terms of stakeholder interactions, wood characterisation and classification, wood valorisation and management technologies, data sharing principles, and impact assessment methods. We will produce the requirements for a comprehensive framework for Construction & Demolition Waste (CDW) cascade valorisation and recycled wood management, drawing on the principles of multi-dimensional cascade valorisation by identifying the different cascade value chain stakeholders and their interactions. A harmonised framework for recycled wood classification will be developed, considering the different schemes available in EU countries. The requirements for an integrated wood cascade valorisation framework will be developed, proposing a set of technologies (related to waste management and digitalisation), and considering non-technological aspects (policy, market, skills, standardisation, certification). We will utilise the multi-dimensional approach to consider the time, value, and function of recycled wood throughout its lifecycle, with a focus on maximising its economic, social, and environmental benefits while minimising its negative impacts. Link to Key Exploitable Results (KERs): KER17 - KER24 Relation to the call: “Increase the use of secondary resources in the process industry […] reduction of CO2 emissions; reduction of waste sent to landfill and overall positive environmental impact.”; “energy efficiency techno-economic and life-cycle assessment considerations of the overall process.” KPI O1.1: To produce a harmonised framework for wood classification in Europe published in a policy brief. KPI O1.3: To develop 8 technologies supporting the implementation of the developed framework. KPI O1.3: To develop a data sharing model and the respective tools among all the identified stakeholders. KPI O1.4: To validate the sustainability performance of the designed framework based on techno-economic and Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) considerations. O2: To improve the separation capabilities of wood sorting mechanisms in line with environmental and technical valorisation parameters. We will develop an advanced separation and sorting system for optimising the cascade valorisation of wood particles. Our project will focus on determining the optimal particle size for efficient and effective valorisation, with the aim of maximising the value and utility of the material while minimising its environmental impact. An adaptive sorting system based on cascade valorisation thresholds will be developed to enable the efficient and automated separation of wood particles based on their quality and potential uses. We will utilise human-robot collaboration and mixed reality technology to improve the accuracy and speed of the separation and sorting process, enabling more efficient and effective management of waste materials. Our project will also provide a set of guidelines and recommendations for the development and implementation of advanced separation and sorting 2 Besserer, A.; Troilo, S.; Girods, P.; Rogaume, Y.; Brosse, N., Cascading Recycling of Wood Waste: A Review. Polymers 2021, 13, 1752, https://doi.org/10.3390/polym13111752 4 systems for the cascade valorisation of wood particles, with the potential to contribute to the broader goal of Associated with document Ref. Ares(2023)8305270 - 05/12/2023 promoting a circular economy and sustainable waste management practices. Link to KERs: KER1 - KER3 Relation to the call: “The proposed technologies should contribute to the matching of supply-versus-demand of feedstock at the level of quality constraints.”; “Considering the upgrading of secondary resources which include development of better separation and sorting technologies.” KPI O2.1: To achieve a 95% accuracy of wood separation from mixed CDW sources. KPI O2.2: To adoptively sort wood based on dynamically updated classification criteria with 90% accuracy. KPI O2.3: To allow collaboration between Robots and humans with non-latency constraints. O3: To develop sustainable and innovative technologies for upcycling waste materials, including cascade refinement, chemical and bioremediation processes, and energy, gas and ashes valorisation, to promote resource efficiency, waste reduction, and sustainable waste management. By encompassing three main themes, namely cascade refinement, chemical and bioremediation processes, and energy/gas/ashes valorisation, we aim to promote resource efficiency, waste reduction, and the development of a circular economy through the sustainable and scalable cascade valorisation of resources coming from C&D wood waste, the development of advanced chemical and bioremediation processes for additive removal, and the exploration of innovative approaches for energy and gas valorisation, in cases where material recovery is not possible. By developing these innovative approaches and technologies, we seek to contribute to a more sustainable future by reducing environmental impacts, promoting circularity, and enabling informed decision-making in waste management practices. Link to KERs: KER4 - KER11 Relation to the call: “Ensure consistent quality and safety of recyclates and their suitability for the upcycling process itself.”; “Detection and removal additives in the secondary resources stream.” KPI O3.1: At least 3 value chains are developed and validated end to end including all the relevant stakeholders. KPI O3.2: The replication potential of the developed technologies to be performed in at least one more sector. O4: To develop innovative approaches and digital tools for enabling the integration of life cycle thinking and advanced sustainability assessments throughout the cascade process. We will develop tools that will enable stakeholders to perform sustainability assessments of the full wood cascade process, taking into account closed loops of materials processed by a variety of available technologies, and considering supply chain limitations from feedstock to end use. This will make it possible for industry and policy stakeholders to identify trade-offs between alternative cascade paths and allow for improved, evidence-based decision-making. We will develop an LCA-based tool to predict and assess the impact of different cascade paths or sequences for possible further uses of wood products and their materials. We will also develop a Supply Chain management tool for the optimisation of upcycling of secondary materials. The use of these tools will be supported by a Digital Product Passport (DPP), containing detailed information about wood-containing product's materials, components, and environmental impact throughout their lifecycle, and a multi-interface digital platform for circularity/upcycling information exchange that will enable relevant stakeholders (businesses and/or consumers) to share information about the environmental impact of products. Link to KERs: KER12 - KER16 Relation to the call: “Foster data sharing, and FAIR digital assets principles, considering the application of DPP between recycling companies and the process industry to improve the economy of scale in upcycling of material streams.”; “Successful
